name: Regin
description: Regin is a skilled smith and dwarf from Norse mythology, best known as the foster father and mentor to the hero Sigurd. He is the son of Hreidmarr and brother to Fafnir and Ótr. Regin's defining characteristic is his cunning and greed, which ultimately leads to his downfall. His most significant role lies in manipulating Sigurd into slaying Fafnir, who had transformed into a dragon to guard the cursed treasure Andvari's hoard. Regin crafts the mighty sword Gram for Sigurd, knowing that it is the only weapon capable of piercing Fafnir's hide. After Sigurd kills the dragon, Regin instructs him to roast Fafnir's heart, planning to eat it himself and gain the dragon's power. However, Sigurd tastes the heart and gains the ability to understand birdsong, learning of Regin's treachery. Forewarned, Sigurd beheads Regin, claiming the treasure for himself and fulfilling his destiny. Regin's story serves as a cautionary tale about greed and the corrupting influence of power, while also highlighting the importance of wisdom and understanding in the face of deceit. He is a pivotal figure in the Volsunga Saga, shaping the events that lead to the tragic downfall of the Volsung clan.
subtitle: Cunning Smith, Mentor, and Betrayer

companions.name: Sigurd
companions.description: Sigurd is Regin's foster son and apprentice. Regin mentors him in smithing and goads him into killing Fafnir to obtain the treasure.

companions.name: Fafnir
companions.description: Fafnir is Regin's brother who was transformed into a dragon. Regin desires Fafnir's hoard of treasure and uses Sigurd to slay him.

companions.name: Hreidmarr
companions.description: Hreidmarr is Regin's father. The initial theft of Andvari's gold, which leads to the family's curse and ultimate downfall, originates with him.

companions.name: Ótr
companions.description: Ótr is Regin's brother who is killed by Loki. The weregild demanded for Ótr's death sparks the chain of events leading to the curse on Andvari's gold.

keyMyths.title: The Slaying of Fafnir
keyMyths.description: Regin manipulates Sigurd into slaying his brother Fafnir, who guards a vast treasure. He forges the sword Gram for Sigurd to use in the battle.

keyMyths.title: Regin's Treachery
keyMyths.description: After Sigurd kills Fafnir, Regin plots to kill Sigurd and take the treasure for himself. However, Sigurd learns of the betrayal through birdsong.

keyMyths.title: Regin's Death
keyMyths.description: Sigurd, warned by the birds, beheads Regin before Regin can enact his treacherous plan. Sigurd then claims the treasure for himself.

sources.author: Unknown
sources.description: Primary source for Regin's story and his role in Sigurd's life.
sources.text: Primary source for Regin's story and his role in Sigurd's life.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: Provides additional context and details about Norse mythology.
sources.text: Provides additional context and details about Norse mythology.

worshipPractices.name: Craftsman's Devotion
worshipPractices.description: While not directly worshipped, artisans, particularly smiths, may invoke Regin's name for skill and cunning in their craft. This would involve offering small, finely crafted items to the forge or a significant project as a symbolic gesture. It is more akin to seeking inspiration than outright worship.

festivals.name: None
festivals.description: Regin is not typically associated with any specific festivals in Norse paganism due to his character being more of a background figure and not a god or deity.

cultural.modernLegacy: Regin's character archetype as the cunning but ultimately flawed mentor continues to appear in modern fantasy literature and games. He represents the dangers of unchecked ambition and the corrupting influence of greed, serving as a cautionary tale within these narratives. The trope of the skilled craftsman with a hidden agenda is also a lasting echo of his character.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both Regin and Hephaestus are skilled smiths who play a crucial role in crafting powerful items. While Hephaestus is a god and Regin a dwarf, their mastery of metalworking is a key similarity.

Both Daedalus and Regin are master craftsmen known for their ingenuity and skill in creating extraordinary objects. Both are also driven by selfish motivations and experience negative consequences because of their actions.
Goibniu, like Regin, is a divine smith associated with crafting weapons and tools of great power. Although a god, the craftmanship and skills of Goibniu are central to his identity and role.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Represents Regin's skill as a smith and the forge where he crafts weapons and tools.

associations.name: Greed
associations.significance: Regin's insatiable desire for wealth and power drives his actions and ultimately leads to his demise.

associations.name: Serpent
associations.significance: Symbolizes Regin's cunning, treachery, and manipulative nature, mirroring his brother Fafnir who transforms into a dragon.

associations.name: One
associations.significance: Represents Regin's singular focus on achieving his goals, often at the expense of others.

associations.name: Craftsmanship
associations.significance: Highlights Regin's exceptional skills as a smith and his ability to create powerful artifacts.

associations.name: Gray
associations.significance: Represents Regin's ambiguous morality and the morally grey decisions he makes.

associations.name: Metal
associations.significance: Symbolizes Regin's profession as a smith and his connection to the earth's resources.

quests.title: The Slaying of Fafnir
quests.description: Regin, driven by greed, manipulates his foster son Sigurd into slaying Fafnir, who has been transformed into a dragon guarding a vast treasure. Regin forges the legendary sword Gram for Sigurd, ensuring he has the weapon needed for the task. Regin plans to kill Sigurd after, take the treasure, and rule the land.
quests.outcome: Sigurd slays Fafnir, but Regin's treachery is revealed when Sigurd tastes Fafnir's blood and understands the language of birds. The birds warn Sigurd of Regin's plot, and Sigurd ultimately kills Regin.

quests.title: The Forging of Gram
quests.description: Regin, a master smith, is tasked by Odin (disguised as an old man) to reforge the broken sword Gram for Sigurd. The first two attempts fail, but Regin uses the fragments of Sigmund's original sword to forge a blade of unmatched power.
quests.outcome: Regin successfully forges Gram, the sword capable of cleaving an anvil in two, proving its worthiness for a hero such as Sigurd.

quests.title: Ottr's Ransom
quests.description: Regin's brother, Ótr, is accidentally killed by Loki, who demands weregild from Regin's father, Hreidmarr. The weregild is to fill Ótr's otter skin with gold and cover it completely on the outside. Regin watches as Loki acquires the cursed Andvaranaut ring, which is needed to completely cover the otter skin, thus setting the stage for future tragedies.
quests.outcome: Hreidmarr receives the gold and the cursed ring as payment for Ótr's death, but the ring carries a curse that brings misfortune and death to all who possess it. Regin's desire for the gold is sparked.

feats.title: Master Smith of Legend
feats.description: Regin is renowned for his exceptional skills as a blacksmith, capable of crafting weapons of unparalleled quality and strength. His most famous creation is the sword Gram, which is destined to be wielded by a hero.
feats.significance: Regin's skill allows Sigurd to complete his heroic quests. Gram becomes a symbol of heroism.

feats.title: Manipulation of a Hero
feats.description: Regin successfully manipulates the young Sigurd into slaying Fafnir, exploiting Sigurd's desire for glory and his own cunning. He uses his position as a foster father to influence Sigurd's actions.
feats.significance: Demonstrates the power of persuasion and the dangerous consequences of unchecked ambition. Regin's actions set in motion the events of the Volsunga Saga.

feats.title: Hoarding of the Andvari Treasure
feats.description: Regin, through his father Hreidmarr, briefly controlled the Andvari treasure, obtained as weregild for the death of his brother, Otr. This treasure was cursed by the dwarf Andvari, bringing misfortune to all who possessed it.
feats.significance: Highlights the destructive power of greed and the futility of seeking wealth at the expense of morality. The treasure ultimately leads to the downfall of Regin and many others.

extendedContent.title: Early Life & Training
extendedContent.content:
Regin's early life is steeped in tragedy and familial conflict. As the son of Hreidmarr, a wealthy dwarf, he lived with his brothers Fafnir and Ótr. The Andvaranaut ring, cursed by the dwarf Andvari, came into Hreidmarr's possession after Loki was forced to acquire it as wergild for accidentally killing Ótr, who had shape-shifted into an otter. This ring, along with the treasure hoard, ignited a fierce greed within Fafnir, who murdered Hreidmarr to claim it all for himself. Regin, unable to directly confront his powerful brother, focused on developing his skills as a smith and sorcerer. He possessed exceptional craftsmanship, a trait common among dwarves in Norse mythology. 

Regin's training involved mastering the arts of metalworking, weapon crafting, and potentially some forms of seidr (Norse magic). He honed his abilities in secret, biding his time and plotting his revenge against Fafnir. He understood that he could not defeat Fafnir alone, especially after Fafnir transformed into a fearsome dragon to protect his hoard. This led him to seek out a hero he could manipulate into doing his bidding. His knowledge of Norse magic may have been limited, but his cunning intellect and expertise in crafting weapons proved invaluable in his ultimate plan.

The *Völsunga saga* details Regin's role as the foster father and mentor to Sigurd. He took Sigurd into his care, teaching him skills necessary for survival and combat. However, Regin's true motivation was not genuine care but rather to groom Sigurd into a weapon he could wield against Fafnir. This period of training was crucial in shaping Sigurd into the legendary hero he would become, unknowingly fulfilling Regin's vengeful desires. Regin imparted his knowledge and skills, but ultimately used Sigurd as a pawn in his own treacherous game.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Challenges
extendedContent.content:
Regin faced numerous challenges throughout his life, primarily stemming from the curse associated with the Andvaranaut ring and his own insatiable greed. His most significant challenge was overcoming his physical weakness compared to his brother, Fafnir. He couldn't directly confront Fafnir, especially after the latter transformed into a dragon. This forced Regin to rely on cunning and manipulation as his primary weapons. Finding and training a suitable champion to defeat Fafnir became his next significant hurdle.

Regin's biggest challenge was maintaining control over Sigurd. He needed to convince Sigurd to slay Fafnir for him, while simultaneously ensuring that Sigurd wouldn't turn against him afterward. This required a delicate balance of flattery, promises of wealth, and veiled threats. He had to constantly assess Sigurd's loyalty and adjust his tactics accordingly. Creating the perfect sword, Gram, for Sigurd was another hurdle. It had to be strong enough to pierce Fafnir's impenetrable hide, a task that tested Regin's smithing skills to their limit. The reforging of Gram from the shattered pieces of Sigmund's sword is a testament to Regin's skill and dedication to his goal. 

Furthermore, Regin faced an internal challenge: his own inherent greed. The lure of Fafnir's treasure constantly threatened to consume him, potentially leading him to make rash decisions that could jeopardize his plans. His desire for wealth ultimately blinded him to the potential consequences of his actions and, in the end, contributed to his downfall. The *Völsunga saga* portrays Regin as constantly wrestling with his avarice, showcasing the corrupting influence of the cursed hoard.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content:
Regin's legacy is complex and largely negative. He is not remembered as a hero or a benevolent figure, but rather as a symbol of greed, treachery, and manipulation. His actions had a profound impact on the lives of those around him, particularly Sigurd and Fafnir. While he played a crucial role in Sigurd's journey to becoming a legendary hero, his motivations were entirely self-serving, and his influence ultimately led to his own demise.

Regin's influence extends to the broader themes explored in Norse mythology. He serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of unchecked ambition and the corrupting power of wealth. His story highlights the cyclical nature of violence and revenge, as the curse of the Andvaranaut ring continues to plague those who possess it. The *Völsunga saga* uses Regin's character to explore themes of fate, free will, and the consequences of one's choices. His actions set in motion a chain of events that ultimately lead to the downfall of the Völsung clan.

Despite his negative attributes, Regin's exceptional skills as a smith have earned him a certain degree of recognition. The creation of Gram, the legendary sword wielded by Sigurd, is a testament to his craftsmanship. However, even this achievement is tainted by his ulterior motives. He is remembered as a skilled craftsman, but one whose talents were used for nefarious purposes. Ultimately, Regin's legacy is one of cunning, greed, and betrayal, a stark reminder of the darker aspects of Norse mythology.

extendedContent.title: Death & Afterlife
extendedContent.content:
Regin's death is a direct consequence of his own treachery. After Sigurd slays Fafnir, Regin asks Sigurd to roast Fafnir's heart, claiming he wants to eat it. While Sigurd is roasting the heart, he touches it to test if it's cooked. Burning himself, he instinctively puts his finger in his mouth, tasting Fafnir's blood. This gives him the ability to understand the language of birds. The birds warn Sigurd that Regin is planning to kill him and take all the treasure for himself. Forewarned, Sigurd recognizes Regin's true intentions and preemptively strikes, beheading Regin with his own sword, Gram.

There is no specific mention of Regin's afterlife in the primary Norse mythological sources. Given his actions in life, it is unlikely that he would have been welcomed into Valhalla, Odin's hall for slain heroes. Valhalla was reserved for warriors who died bravely in battle, a category Regin certainly did not fit. More likely, he would have ended up in Náströnd, a hall in Niflheim, the realm of the dead, reserved for oathbreakers, murderers, and other evil-doers. Náströnd is described as a hall made of serpents whose venom drips upon the damned souls within.

Although the exact details of Regin's afterlife remain ambiguous, his death serves as a fitting end to his treacherous life. It reinforces the Norse belief that greed and betrayal will ultimately lead to one's own destruction. His demise also highlights the importance of wisdom and foresight, as Sigurd's ability to understand the birds' warning allows him to escape Regin's trap. Regin's death is a pivotal moment in the *Völsunga saga*, marking the completion of Sigurd's first major heroic deed and setting the stage for the subsequent events of the saga.

shortDescription: A cunning dwarf smith in Norse myth who fosters Sigurd and plots to acquire the dragon Fafnir's treasure. He is ultimately slain by Sigurd.
